content:
12 min read
Just now

--

As AI collapses the cost of legal output, coordination becomes the new judgment layer.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Follow the Money: what isn’t being built yet
The list of well-capitalized legaltech companies and AI startups is well known and keeps growing (thank you Jude Law and Harvey Spector), hitting $4.3 billion in 2025 alone [1] [2]. And almost every one of them is building on the same side of legal work: substantive production.
Drafting. Research. Diligence. Review. Summarization… The output side of the lawyer’s day. Yet every output must be reviewed, reconciled, attributed, and trusted before it can move anything forward.
This is the trade most are not accounting for or pricing correctly:
AI reduces execution friction but increases coordination surface area.
That’s the whole game. AI is making it cheap to create work. But it’s also making it more expensive than ever to coordinate work. What outputs are worthy of attention, what needs to be done next, and who (or what) should do it. And almost no one is building for that half of the equation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What AI Actually Changes
In the ABA Center for Innovation, I wrote earlier this year that in times of great change, processes change first and systems change second. The process layer in legal is updating fast: drafting, summarizing, researching, comparing, extracting, analyzing. Every one of those tasks is dramatically faster and cheaper than it was two years ago. More than 90% of lawyers globally now use at least one AI tool in their daily work,[3] saving somewhere between one and ten hours a week.[4] Legal AI use among professionals nearly doubled in a single year.[5]
This is real. This is happening. This is the easy story.
But we’re feeling the downside too. Because AI doesn’t reduce the number of moving parts in your work, it multiplies them.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Think about what the modern professional’s input onslaught looks like in 2026:
• a person
• their team
• their clients
• drafting AI tool(s)
• research AI tool(s)
• AI analysis integrated with a document management system
• internal knowledge systems
• external knowledge systems
• tidal waves of news and new media
• email, docs, chat, calendars
• and a growing number of agents claiming to be teammates
More sources. More to verify. More to track. More to trust.
This is not a hypothetical problem, and the data is beginning to arrive.
The GenAI Divide Is a Coordination Problem
MIT reported that in 2025, somewhere between $30 and $40 billion in enterprise AI spend yielded 95% of organizations reported no measurable return on their AI investments.[6]
95%. Zero return. On forty billion dollars. And there are hundreds of billions of capex losses planned just over the next 18 months from every technology company on the planet.
This is not a story about bad models. The models are extraordinary. The story is about a gap that MIT named precisely: the GenAI Divide, the gap between high adoption and low transformation.[7] One in twenty companies is getting outsized value. The rest are stuck.
What’s spilling out for the other ninety-five percent is what Harvard Business Review calls “Workslop.”[8] Content that looks like good work but lacks the substance to actually advance a task.
Stanford and BetterUp researchers surveyed 1,150 desk workers. 40% had received workslop in the past month. Each instance cost the receiver an average of nearly two hours of rework.[9]
AI output does not arrive as raw material. It arrives looking finished.
Workslop’s most insidious effect is not that it’s bad. It’s that it transfers the burden from creator to receiver.[10] More effort upstream becomes more effort downstream. A shift that, in many ways, is harder to detect.
We have a clear industry-wide pattern: Adoption is up. ROI is flat. Output is up. Trust is down.
This isn’t a model problem. This is a coordination problem.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The New Coordination Stack
Here’s what coordination actually looks like in this new world. It’s three layers, and AI changes all of them.
1. Task decomposition. Who does this: me, a person, or a machine?
This sounds trivial. It is not. The wrong answer wastes the AI’s leverage and your time. There is no good current tool for thinking about this systematically. Most knowledge workers (lawyers in particular) are just figuring it out one task at a time, by feel.
2. Output validation. AI produces drafts, summaries, analyses. Someone has to verify the accuracy, reconcile the inconsistencies, eliminate the hallucinations, and decide whether the product is actually defensible. This is a new form of coordination work. Not coordination between people. Coordination between outputs.
This is the lawyer’s judgment.
This is expertise.
The Shift
Andrej Karpathy, speaking at Sequoia AI Ascent in May 2026, dated the AI inflection point to December 2025, the month coding agents crossed from autocomplete to genuine delegation. He flipped from writing 80% of his own code to delegating 80% to AI agents. His closing line at Sequoia:
“You can outsource your thinking, but you can’t outsource your understanding.”
For lawyers, that distinction is everything.
And we can already see what this looks like in practice. Ramp, the $32B fintech, claims to have hit 99.5% AI adoption across its 1,500 employees this year. Non-engineers in finance, sales, and legal now generate 12% of human-initiated pull requests against Ramp’s production codebase. Their CPO Geoff Charles, in a widely-circulated April 2026 post: “the harness, not the model, is the bottleneck.” Same models everyone else has. Different harness. Wildly different result.
That word, harness, is the one to hold onto. The harness is the substrate around the model: the workflow, the memory, the integrations, the permissions, the review structure. It’s how Karpathy outsources thinking without outsourcing understanding. It’s how Ramp gets non-engineers shipping production code. It’s what most legal AI deployments are missing.
The lawyers who ask the best questions and communicate most clearly will continue to be the most valuable lawyers. That has always been true. And as far as this humble writer can see, it will continue to be true. The form evolves, the substance doesn’t.
But the cost of getting validation wrong is no longer hypothetical. The legal industry has now produced more than 1,300 documented cases of lawyers submitting AI-generated fake citations in court.[11] The pace went from “two cases a week” in early 2025 to “two or three a day” by that fall.[12]
In a recent episode of Zach Ambramowitz’s Legally Disrupted, Zach and Richard Tromans (founder of Artificial Lawyer) reframed the panic helpfully: humans have always hallucinated. Lawyers misremember cases. Witnesses misremember facts. The whole appellate system exists to catch errors. The question isn’t whether AI introduces error into legal work. It’s whether the workflow around it catches the errors before they reach a court.
That reframe lands the right way. Hallucination isn’t just a model failure. It’s a workflow failure. Which means the answer isn’t a smarter model, the answer is a better workflow.
Verification, in other words, is becoming the central part of the standard of care.
3. State management. What’s been done? What’s outstanding? What changed? What’s been reviewed? What’s blocked? Who owns the next move?
Before AI, people held this context. Partners in their heads. Associates in their notes. Clients in their inboxes.
Now, for the first time, the system has the opportunity to house this context.
This is the part most people miss, and the most consequential. Once AI is producing drafts and analyses faster than humans can read them, the durable system of record for a matter is no longer the document. It’s the graph: the relationships among issues, facts, tasks, documents, people, decisions, risks, deadlines, and outputs.
What lives in the graph is what used to live in someone’s head. The fact that the indemnification position changed last Tuesday after a partner call. The reason the disclosure schedule got reorganized. The open question about regulatory approval that nobody has formally owned yet. The dependency between two workstreams that means nothing in stream B can ship until stream A clears. None of that is in the document. It’s in the graph.
Without that graph, AI doesn’t accelerate matters. It fragments them.
Why This Is Sharpest in Legal
Every knowledge industry is going to live through some version of this. But legal feels it earliest and hardest, for a reason that’s been true forever:
Legal work was always coordination work
Block CEO Jack Dorsey and Sequoia’s Roelof Botha put it more bluntly in March: hierarchy itself was always an information-routing protocol, and AI is the first technology powerful enough to replace it.
Going back to the Roman Army’s command structure of 8–80–480–5000 (8 generals control 80 chiefs who command 480 legionnaires who were in turn in charge of 5000 soldiers), formalized by the Prussians after Napoleon, inherited by every modern corporation through the railroads. The constraint has never changed: one human leader can effectively manage between three and eight people. So organizations built layers to compensate. More layers, slower information.
Dorsey’s argument: AI is the first technology powerful enough to actually replace what hierarchy does. Block is rebuilding itself around an internal “world model” instead of an org chart. Every decision, every artifact, every transaction feeds a continuously updated picture of the company. Coordination stops being something humans do in meetings. It becomes something the system maintains in the background.
What they’re describing at the company level is exactly what’s about to happen at the matter level. The matter graph is to a deal what Block’s world model is to a company: the substrate that holds the work together when humans alone can’t.
Lawyers like to describe their job as research, drafting, and advice. That’s true, but inside an actual matter, the job looks different.
A lawyer running an M&A deal, a litigation, or an investigation is coordinating clients, partners, associates, opposing counsel, experts, deadlines, regulation and approvals. All at once. All changing.
A matter is not a task. It’s a living system of people, facts, documents, deadlines, judgments, and dependencies.
This is not administrative residue. This is the legal product.
The data backs it up, painfully:
• Lawyers spend just 2.9 hours per day on substantive billable work.[13]
• 49% of legal professionals spend seven or more hours a week on administrative or non-specialized work.[14]
• Only 20% of legal matters sent to outside counsel stay within budget. Lawyers who actively manage matters are twice as likely to keep them on track.[15]
The thing legal AI is racing to automate, drafting, research, summarization, is important and already working. The part that was always broken. The stage breaking now is coordination, and the solution has barely been touched.
From Managing People to Managing Systems
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Senior lawyers used to coordinate junior lawyers doing work.
Increasingly, they’ll coordinate systems producing work.
The lawyer’s value isn’t in personally producing every artifact. It’s in:
• decomposing the work
• routing it to the right human or machine
• validating outputs
• reconciling inconsistencies
• managing dependencies
• preserving context
• making the judgment calls
• staying accountable
I called this in my last piece the lawyer-as-conductor: that we need to be conductors of our own symphonies. McKinsey is now using nearly identical language across the broader economy. Their 2025 work talks about “human-agent partnerships” and predicts that by 2030, the new core workplace skill will be effective interact
